facts.fact: A write capacity unit (WCU) in DynamoDB represents one write per second for an item up to 1 KB; item sizes are rounded up to the next 1 KB multiple.
DynamoDB on-demand mode automatically accommodates up to double your previous peak traffic within a 30-minute window; larger scaling events require 30+ minutes planning to avoid throttling.
For each 1K tokens in an LLM context, ~2.6GB of additional memory is required; at 16K context, KV cache for a single request consumes ~40GB.
Prefill latency for million-token contexts using Cascading KV Cache is 6.8× faster than Flash Attention 2, taking only 14.8% of quadratic method time.
KV cache for 1M tokens requires ~15GB per user, making million-token context windows production-ready with modern infrastructure (Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4, Qwen2.5-1M).
In most real-world systems, the read-to-write ratio is heavily skewed towards reads, starting at 10:1 but often reaching 100:1 or higher for content-heavy applications.
Stripe handles 500+ million API requests daily with 5 million database queries per second across 2,000+ shards at 99.999% availability.
Netflix processes approximately 5.8k QPS average from 250 million daily active users with 2 sessions per user per day (~500 million play starts), with bursts reaching 4–5× average.
Netflix's Open Connect CDN achieves approximately 98% cache hit rate, reducing origin traffic to just 2% of edge traffic.
Twitter (with 300M monthly active users, 50% daily active) generates ~3,500 QPS for tweets with peak load of ~7,000 QPS; favorites run ~30k QPS and reads ~75k QPS.
Twitter 500M tweets daily at 300 bytes per tweet = 140GB daily or 50TB annually; media at 100× tweet size = 10TB daily or 4PB annually.
Uber expects 200k location update/writes per second and 200k queries per second, doubling during peak hours; 20M ride requests daily (~5,000 events/sec).
Peak QPS multiplier varies by application: 2× for predictable SaaS, 5–10× for bursty apps (ride-sharing during rush hour), 100× for e-commerce flash sales.
Bandwidth calculation formula: Mbps = (data per operation in megabits) × (operations per second); convert bytes to bits using ×8 multiplier.
Real-world network throughput is 70–90% of theoretical maximum due to protocol overhead, congestion, and latency; apply 1.3–2× safety margin to base estimates.
LSM-trees handle 5–10× more writes per second than B-trees on same hardware, converting random writes to sequential writes; B-trees minimize read latency.
B-tree point lookups are faster; LSM-tree point lookups may check memtable plus multiple levels of SSTables, with keys from long ago requiring deep file traversal.
Surrogate keys reduce foreign key complexity and perform better on multi-column joins, but lose business semantics; natural keys avoid extra storage but require immutability.
Composite primary keys are ideal for junction tables in many-to-many relationships; surrogate keys for composite natural keys often violate 3NF but improve performance.
High cardinality in junction tables without proper design causes data duplication, incorrect analytics, and long-term maintenance pain in production systems.
Stateless services enable true horizontal scaling; stateful services require session affinity (sticky sessions) or external state management (Redis) for horizontal scale.
CAP theorem states at most two of {Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance} can be guaranteed; partition tolerance is mandatory, forcing choice between consistency or availability.
CP systems prioritize consistency, rejecting requests during partitions; AP systems prioritize availability but serve stale data until eventual sync.
Average QPS = (DAU × actions per user per day) ÷ 86,400 seconds; peak QPS = average QPS × peak multiplier (typically 2–10× based on application type).
Storage estimation: multiply data per operation × operations per day × retention period × replication factor (typically 3×); long-term retention can multiply estimates by 100+.

examples.example: Stripe scales to 5 million QPS across 2,000 MongoDB shards, processing 500+ million API requests daily, handling ~$1 trillion in annual payment volume at 99.999% uptime.
Netflix handles 250 million daily active users, 500 million play starts daily (~5.8k QPS average), with Open Connect CDN achieving 98% cache hit rate.
examples.metric: 250M DAU, 5.8k QPS average, 98% cache hit rate

examples.example: Twitter (X) with 300M monthly active users, 50% daily active, generates 500M tweets daily (140GB), creating ~3.5k QPS average with peak 7k QPS.
Uber processes 200k location updates/sec, 200k queries/sec, doubling during peak hours, with surge pricing driven by real-time supply-demand geospatial partitioning.
examples.metric: 200k QPS reads, 200k QPS writes, 5x peak multiplier

examples.example: DynamoDB on-demand mode automatically handles 2× previous peak traffic within 30 minutes; beyond that requires advance planning to avoid throttling.

analogies.analogy: Stateless services are like checkout counters in a grocery store—any clerk can help any customer. Stateful services are like reserved tables at a restaurant—customers return to the same place. Stateless scales horizontally; stateful requires session affinity glue.

A B-tree is like a phone book—you flip to a page, scan a few entries, find the number. An LSM tree is like a mail sorter—unsorted mail goes into a pile (memtable), gets sorted in batches, and merged into folders (levels). B-trees are fast for lookups; LSM trees are fast for bulk writes.

CAP theorem is like a supply chain: Consistency = all stores have the same price, Availability = every store is open, Partition Tolerance = stores can operate offline. During a network outage, you can't have all three—choose price uniformity or stay open with stale prices.

A junction table in many-to-many relationships is like a theater ticket log—it records which student attended which concert, with each ticket a separate row. Without it, you'd try to store a list of concerts in each student row (cardinality explosion) and a list of students in each concert row (duplication).

Peak QPS to average QPS is like highway traffic: your freeway is designed for rush hour (5–10× normal volume), not the quiet midnight drive. Designing for average load means your system fails exactly when users need it most.

key_numbers.context: Seconds per day; dividing daily requests by 86,400 gives average QPS (the baseline before peak multiplier)
DynamoDB write capacity unit threshold; items > 1 KB are rounded up to next 1 KB multiple
KV cache memory per 1K tokens in LLM inference; 16K tokens = ~40GB
KV cache size for 1M token context window per user; production-ready threshold
Read-to-write ratio; typical range from 10:1 (balanced) to 100:1+ (content-heavy)
Stripe's production database query capacity across MongoDB shards
Netflix daily active users from 2+ billion subscriber base
Netflix Open Connect CDN cache hit rate, reducing origin traffic to 2%
Twitter daily tweet volume; 140GB/day storage or ~50TB annually
Peak QPS multiplier for predictable apps (SaaS); bursty apps use 5–10×, e-commerce flash sales use 100×
Realistic network throughput as percentage of theoretical maximum; apply 1.3–2× safety margin
LSM-tree write throughput advantage over B-trees on same hardware
Standard data replication for durability; multiplies storage by 3×
Stripe MongoDB shard count to support 5M QPS
DynamoDB on-demand mode auto-scaling window; beyond 2× previous peak requires advance planning

common_misconceptions.misconception: Designing for average QPS is sufficient; peak load is predictable
common_misconceptions.reality: Average QPS × peak multiplier is critical. Designing for average load causes failures exactly when users notice most. Peak multiplier ranges from 2× (SaaS) to 100× (flash sales) based on application type.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Read-to-write ratio is roughly balanced (1:1) for most systems
common_misconceptions.reality: Most systems are read-heavy, starting at 10:1 and reaching 100:1+ for content-heavy apps. This fundamentally changes scaling strategy (read replicas vs. sharding).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Stateful services (with session affinity) scale horizontally as easily as stateless
common_misconceptions.reality: Stateful services require sticky sessions or external state management (Redis), adding latency and complexity. Stateless services enable true horizontal scaling without load balancer affinity.

common_misconceptions.misconception: You can always choose Consistency, Availability, AND Partition Tolerance (all three CAP guarantees)
common_misconceptions.reality: CAP theorem requires choosing at most two. Partition tolerance is mandatory (networks fail), forcing a choice between consistency (stale reads during outage) or availability (unavailable during partition).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Natural keys are always better because they have business meaning
common_misconceptions.reality: Surrogate keys are often better in high-scale systems. Natural keys can change (email addresses, SSNs), are larger in indexes, and complicate joins when composite. Surrogate keys trade business semantics for flexibility and performance.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Using a list of related IDs in a single column (non-normalized) saves space compared to a junction table
common_misconceptions.reality: Non-normalized many-to-many designs cause cardinality explosion, data duplication, and query complexity. Junction tables are properly normalized and prevent anomalies.

common_misconceptions.misconception: B-trees are always better for databases because lookups are fast
common_misconceptions.reality: LSM-trees outperform B-trees on write-heavy workloads (5–10× throughput), making them ideal for time-series and event logs. Databases choose B-trees (MySQL, Postgres) for read-heavy, LSM-trees (RocksDB, Cassandra) for write-heavy.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Bandwidth = data size / time; 1 Mbps means 1 MB per second
common_misconceptions.reality: Bandwidth is in megabits per second (Mbps); you must divide by 8 to get megabytes per second (MB/s). A 100 Mbps connection = 12.5 MB/s.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Network throughput is the same as advertised bandwidth
common_misconceptions.reality: Real-world throughput is 70–90% of theoretical maximum due to protocol overhead, congestion, and duplex losses. Always apply a 1.3–2× safety margin to base estimates.

common_misconceptions.misconception: LLM context window size scales linearly with memory
common_misconceptions.reality: KV cache memory grows at ~2.6 GB per 1K tokens; 1M-token context requires ~15 GB per user. Prefill latency scales quadratically without optimization (6.8× speedup with Cascading KV Cache).
